results.title: [Literature Review] Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal ...
results.content:
CONTEXTRL is a post-training method that augments standard RL (specifically, GRPO) with an auxiliary context-selection objective. Instead of solely rewarding the final answer's correctness, CONTEXTRL also rewards the model for explicitly selecting the context that supports a given query-answer pair. This is achieved by constructing contrastive context pairs $(C^+, C^-)$ for a given query $Q$ and anchor answer $A$, where $C^+$ is the supporting context and $C^-$ is a minimally perturbed, confounding alternative that supports a different answer.

The contrastive data construction is detailed for two domains:

`<PATCH_MASKED>` [...] Moonlight Logo
Moonlight Logo
Moonlight Logo

# [Literature Review] Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal LLMs

The paper introduces CONTEXTRL, a context-aware reinforcement learning (RL) framework designed to address the common failure mode of large language models (LLMs) in grounding their predictions in sparse yet decisive contextual evidence. This "context unawareness" leads to locally plausible but context-inconsistent decisions, particularly in long-horizon reasoning for agentic systems and fine-grained perception in multimodal models. [...] The Context-Aware Reinforcement Learning methodology integrates this auxiliary objective into GRPO. For a contrastive instance $z = (Q, A, C^+, C^-)$, the model is prompted with $Q$, $A$, and the two contexts as labeled options ($t^+$ for $C^+$, $t^-$ for $C^-$). The context-awareness loss, $L\_{CA}$, is defined as:
$L\_{CA}(z; \theta) = - \log \sigma (\text{clip} (\Delta\_\theta(z), -c, c))$

# In-context learning as an alternative to RL training - I implemented Stanford's ACE framework for agents that learn from execution feedback : r/reinforcementlearning
Skip to main contentIn-context learning as an alternative to RL training - I implemented Stanford's ACE framework for agents that learn from execution feedback : r/reinforcementlearning

Open menu Open navigation, basically achieving RL-style improvement purely through context management.

How it works:

Agent runs task → reflects on execution trace (successes/failures) → curates strategies into playbook → injects playbook as context on next run

So.. it’s just test time scaling?

 Reply  Share 

[](

cheetguy

•7mo ago

Not quite, ACE is about learning across multiple runs by building a persistent playbook of strategies. The agent reflects after each task and curates what worked into reusable patterns. So it's cross-task learning through context management rather than single-task compute scaling.

 Reply  Share

results.title: Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal LLMs - arXiv
results.content:
RL by introducing perception-aware reward shaping, as a baseline. Notably, PAPO is trained on its own curated dataset and uses a different RL formulation, making it a strong but non-directly comparable reference point. Including it helps contextualize the magnitude of gains achievable through alternative approaches. [...] As illustrated in Figure 3, we first construct contrastive context pairs (C+,C−)(C^{+},C^{-}) across both agentic and multimodal settings. Each pair is associated with a query QQ and an anchor answer AA, where C+C^{+} is the supporting context for AA and C−C^{-} is a minimally perturbed but confounding alternative that instead supports a different answer. The context takes the form of an agent trajectory τ\tau for agentic coding, or an image II for multimodal tasks. We then jointly optimize the standard GRPO objective on the original task data and a context-awareness loss ℒCA\mathcal{L}\_{\mathrm{CA}} on these contrastive pairs, which conditions on (Q,A)(Q,A) and rewards the model for selecting C+C^{+} over C−C^{-}. [...] Motivated by this observation, we propose Context-Aware Reinforcement Learning (ContextRL), a post-training method that augments reinforcement learning (RL) with explicit context-selection supervision. Whereas standard RL optimizes final answers through outcome rewards, ContextRL adds an auxiliary objective that rewards the model for selecting the context that supports a given answer.
As illustrated in Figure˜3, we construct contrastive context pairs through condition search, generative editing, and similarity-based retrieval, and incorporate them into standard GRPO post-training (Shao et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2025) via a logit-level contrastive loss that favors the context aligned with the ground-truth answer.

Refer to caption

results.title: [PDF] Widening the Pipeline in Human-Guided Reinforcement Learning ...
results.content:
EXPAND employs a novel context-aware data augmentation method, which ampliﬁes the dif-ference between relevant and irrelevant regions in human explanation by applying multiple perturbations to irrelevant parts. To reduce the human effort needed to provide visual explanations, EXPAND uses off-the-shelf object detectors and trackers to allow humans to to give explanations in terms of salient objects in the visual state, which are then automatically converted to saliency regions to be used by the RL system. We show that EXPAND agents require fewer interactions with the environment (environment sample efﬁciency) and over 30% fewer human signals (human feedback sample efﬁciency) by leveraging human visual explanation. [...] The bottom image shows an example image used by EXPAND which contrastively highlights the taxi and passenger by performing a Gaussian blur over the remainder of the image observation. This illustrates why the proposed context-aware augmentation can be more informative than standard data augmentations.
We choose to use Gaussian blurring to perturb the irrelevant regions, which is also used in a previous Explainable RL work . The reason we use Gaussian blurring is that, it effectively perturbs objects in image while does not introduce new information. One counterexample here can be, transformations like cutout might signiﬁcantly change state content in environments where small black blocks have particular semantic meaning. [...] 4 Method EXPAND aims to improve data efﬁciency and performance with explanation augmented feedback. In the following, we will ﬁrst describe how EXPAND simultaneously learns from both environment reward and binary evaluative feedback. Then we introduce our novel context-aware data augmentation that utilizes domain knowledge with human explanation. Algorithm 1 in Appendix A presents the train-interaction loop of EXPAND. Within an episode, the agent interacts with the environment and stores its transition experiences. Every few episodes, it collects human feedback queried on a trajectory sampled from the most recent policy. All the human feedback is stored in a feedback buffer similar to the replay buffer in off-policy DRL . The weights of RL agent are updated twice, ﬁrst using sampled

results.title: Context Aware Policy Adaptation: Towards Robust Safe Reinforcement Learning | OpenReview
results.content: Abstract: Real world tasks are often safety-critical, requiring policies that respect safety constraints while also being able to safely adapt to novel situations. Typical safe reinforcement learning methods focus on adapting to shifts in the transition function but assume a fixed state space, limiting their ability to generalize to novel states. We consider the problem of safe reinforcement learning that must adapt to novel, potentially unsafe states. Our proposed approach for context aware policy adaptation leverages foundation models as a contextual representation that enables the agent to align novel observations with similar experience. We demonstrate empirically that our approach is able to generalize across different types of novelty that may include dangerous as well as safe
